nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reefs and reef flats; found across a wide depth range from intertidal pools down to mesophotic depths, with most aquarium specimens collected in 1–30 m.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with established biofilm

diet.notes: In the wild, grazes on bacterial films, microalgae, sponges, and detritus across reef rock. In captivity, survival depends on a mature system with abundant natural biofilm; many specimens slowly starve in newer tanks. Some keepers supplement with small pieces of clam or shrimp placed near the star, but it cannot reliably be trained onto prepared foods.

avoidWith: Harlequin shrimp (Hymenocera picta — obligate sea-star predator)
small clams and sponges (may be grazed)

summary: The Multi-color Linckia Sea Star (Linckia multifora) is a small, variably-colored Indo-Pacific sea star with a reputation for high captive mortality. It is generally considered suitable only for experienced reefkeepers with a mature, biofilm-rich system.
careNotes: The Multi-color Linckia Sea Star is the smaller, more variably-colored relative of the better-known Blue Linckia (Linckia laevigata). It is commonly traded under several names — Multi-color, Orange, Red, Mottled, or Dalmatian Linckia — reflecting the wide range of colors this species displays, from pale orange and yellow to deep red and mottled brown. The Caribbean Comet Star (Linckia guildingii) is a closely related species with effectively identical care requirements. Despite visually attractive coloration, this sea star is widely considered one of the more challenging invertebrates to keep successfully in captivity: shipping stress, sudden parameter changes, and immature tank biofilms commonly cause slow tissue necrosis — white patches that spread across the body until the animal dies. Only mature, established reef systems (at minimum 12 months old) with very stable parameters provide sufficient biofilm and microfaunal diversity to sustain this species long-term. An extended drip acclimation of 2–4 hours is generally advised upon arrival, and the animal must never be exposed to air during transfer. Copper-based medications are lethal to sea stars — never use them in any system housing a Linckia. The species is notable for autotomy: arms readily detach and can regenerate into new individuals, a behavior that gives rise to the trade name 'Comet Sea Star'. The Multi-color Linckia is generally considered reef-safe, though isolated reports of grazing on small clams, sponges, and zoanthids have been noted.
breedingNotes: Reproduces both sexually (broadcast spawning) and asexually via autotomy — detached arms regenerate a new disk and four additional arms over roughly 10 months, producing a 'comet' juvenile. Not deliberately bred in aquaria, but spontaneous arm detachment is common and regeneration in a stable mature system is documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: LiveAquaria does not list a dedicated 'Multi-color Linckia' product page; the Red Linckia listing (which is the same species, L. multifora, sold under a color-specific trade name) was used as the primary care reference.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Designated secondary; product page lists this animal as 'Linckia sp.' (orange morph) with minimal husbandry detail ('description coming soon'). Confirmed Indo-Pacific origin and the basic parameter envelope (74–78°F / 23.3–25.6°C, pH 8.2–8.4, reef-safe), but lacked size, tank-size, and care-level data — fell back to two additional sources for cross-verification.

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Substitute secondary used because Aqua Imports lacked husbandry detail. Confirmed scientific name Linckia multifora, care level 'Difficult — requires mature aquarium', 75+ gallon minimum, temperature 23.9–25°C, pH 8.1–8.3, salinity 1.024–1.026, lifespan 5+ years, peaceful temperament, slow drip acclimation requirement, and starvation as the main mortality cause.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (22–26°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.025), kH (8–12), adult size ~15 cm general Linckia, care level 'Difficult', and copper sensitivity. Confirms color variation in shades of yellow, brown, orange, and red.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Linckia multifora)
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority: family Ophidiasteridae, order Valvatida. Confirmed circumtropical Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea, Indian Ocean, western and central Pacific north to Japan, south to Lord Howe, east to Pitcairn, northeast to Hawaii), depth range to ~213 m, adult diameter 5–13 cm (smaller than L. laevigata), and autotomy-driven 'comet' regeneration.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Linckia sea stars are notorious for high captive mortality. Even in well-established reef tanks, many specimens slowly starve or develop tissue necrosis from shipping stress and parameter instability. Recommended only for experienced reefkeepers with a mature, biofilm-rich system who can perform extended drip acclimation.
